title: Plan a Stage 5 Mathematics Exhibition
overview: Bring together everything from Stage 5 Mathematics to design a mathematics exhibition that teaches visitors the most important ideas from this stage.

project.drivingQuestion: How can we use the mathematics from Stage 5 to plan a clear, accurate and welcoming exhibition for our school or community?
project.finalProduct: Create an exhibition plan with labelled displays, a schedule, a budget, a visitor survey with a data display, and a short explanation of your mathematical decisions.

stages.title: 1. Term 1 foundations display
stages.prompt: Choose the two most important ideas from Term 1 (Units 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6). Build a display that teaches each idea with a model, an example and a check question.
stages.evidence: Two labelled displays with models, examples and check questions

stages.title: 2. Term 2 applications corner
stages.prompt: Design an activity corner where visitors use Term 2 skills (Units 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12) to solve one real problem, showing every step of the method.
stages.evidence: A worked real problem with each method step shown

stages.title: 3. Term 3 connections wall
stages.prompt: Create a connections wall that links Term 3 ideas (Units 13, 14, 15, 16, 17, 18) to earlier units, with at least three labelled connections.
stages.evidence: A connections wall with three labelled links between units

stages.title: 4. Present and explain
stages.prompt: Present your exhibition plan. Explain at least three mathematical choices, check that your answers are sensible and identify one improvement you would make.
stages.evidence: Spoken, written or recorded mathematical explanation

evidenceChecklist: A worked real problem with method steps
A connections wall with three links
A schedule and budget for the exhibition
A survey with organised data display

rubric.secure: Calculations, measures, shapes, data and models are accurate and checked.
The plan connects ideas from several Stage 5 units for a clear purpose.
Labels, diagrams, tables or charts make the mathematics visible.
Decisions are explained using appropriate mathematical words and evidence.

questions.question: Round 14,205 to the nearest 100.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 14205 rounds to 14,200 — look at the tens digit.

questions.question: Round 74,579 to the nearest 10.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 74579 rounds to 74,580 — look at the ones digit.

questions.question: How many degrees are there in a full turn?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: How many vertices does a cube have?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: Find the next term: 6, 12, 18, 24, ___
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: The rule is add 6 each time, so the next term is 24 + 6 = 30.

questions.question: If x = 6, what is the value of 3x + 3?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: Here is some data: 16, 17, 12, 3, 9, 16. Which value appears most often (the mode)?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 16 appears more than any other value, so it is the mode.

questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 2/4 simplifies to 1/2, so they are equivalent.

questions.question: How many sides does a pentagon have?

questions.question: How many degrees are there in a right angle?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: Calculate 274 × 6.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 11 methods.
questions.explanation: Estimate: 274 rounds to 270, and 270 × 6 = 1620. Now work through the columns. Ones: 4 × 6 = 24, write 4, carry 2. Tens: 7 × 6 = 42, plus the carried 2 = 44, write 4, carry 4. Hundreds: 2 × 6 = 12, plus the carried 4 = 16. The answer is…

questions.question: Calculate 67 × 24.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 11 methods.
questions.explanation: Estimate: 70 × 24 = 1680. Multiply by the ones: 67 × 4 = 268. Multiply by the tens: 67 × 20 = 1340 (write the place-holding zero, then 67 × 2 = 134). Add the partial products: 268 + 1340 = 1608. This is close to 1680, so we are confident.

questions.question: A tally shows these counts: 18, 8, 4, 1, 17. What is the total?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: Here is some data: 12, 15, 14, 18, 8, 12. Which value appears most often (the mode)?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 12 appears more than any other value, so it is the mode.

questions.question: What is 1/4 of 32?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: Which fraction is larger: 1/2 or 1/4?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: Halves are bigger than quarters, so 1/2 is larger than 1/4.

questions.question: A rectangle is 6 cm wide and 7 cm tall. What is its perimeter in centimetres?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: How many grams are in 3 kilograms?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: Calculate 1/8 × 6.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 15 methods.
questions.explanation: Keep the denominator (eighths) and multiply the numerator: 1 × 6 = 6. So 1/8 × 6 = 6/8, which simplifies to 3/4. Picture a bar in eighths with six parts shaded, and you can see it fills three quarters of the bar.
questions.unitTitle: Multiplying and Dividing Fractions and Decimals

questions.question: Calculate 1/9 × 12.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 15 methods.
questions.explanation: 1 × 12 = 12, so the answer is 12/9. Because 12 is bigger than 9, this is more than one whole. Rewrite it: 12/9 = 1 3/9 = 1 1/3. Multiplying a fraction can take you past one whole, and that is perfectly fine.
questions.unitTitle: Multiplying and Dividing Fractions and Decimals

questions.question: How many minutes are there in 4 hours?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: A lesson starts at 8 o'clock and lasts 3 hours. At what hour does it end?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: Work out 6 + 79 the easy way.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 17 methods.
questions.explanation: Counting on 79 from 6 is slow, so swap the order: 6 + 79 = 79 + 6. Now count on 6 from 79: 79 + 6 = 85. The commutative law let us start from the larger number.
questions.unitTitle: Number and the Laws of Arithmetic

questions.question: Work out 50 × 17 × 2.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 17 methods.
questions.explanation: Look for a friendly pair. 50 × 2 = 100. Regroup as (50 × 2) × 17 = 100 × 17 = 1,700. Grouping the 50 and 2 first turned a hard sum into an easy one.
questions.unitTitle: Number and the Laws of Arithmetic
